question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject CRLF sequences into the Link response header by crafting a request whose pagination `links.next` value contains `\\r\\n` characters, since `linkNextHeaderValue` performs no sanitization before embedding `linksNext` into `<${linksNext}>; rel=\

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject arbitrary HTTP headers by supplying a `links.next` URL containing `\\r\\nSet-Cookie: session=attacker` since `linkNextHeaderValue` directly interpolates the unsanitized `linksNext` value into the header string passed to `res.set(LINK_NEXT_HEADER, ...)`, leading to session fixation or cookie injection against other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause log injection by crafting a URL (`req.originalUrl`) containing newline characters (`%0a`, `%0d`) since line 57 logs `req.originalUrl` directly via `logger.info(\\`${req.ip} ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl} in ${elapsed} ms: ${code}\\`)` without sanitization, allowing the attacker to forge fake log entries that impersonate other IPs or methods?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user spoof the source IP in log entries by sending requests with a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header, since `req.ip` on line 57 may reflect the proxy-forwarded IP without validation, causing security analysts to investigate the wrong source during incident response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the application logs by repeatedly triggering 400-level responses (e.g., malformed requests), since line 56 unconditionally logs every request where `code >= 400`, enabling a log-based denial-of-service that exhausts disk space or log aggregation capacity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the probabilistic `sampleRate` logging filter entirely by crafting requests that always produce `code >= 400`, since the condition `code >= 400 || Math.random() <= sampleRate` short-circuits on the first operand, guaranteeing every error request is logged and enabling targeted log flooding?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a prototype pollution attack via the `headers.path[path]` lookup on line 36, where `path` is derived from `res.locals[requestPathLabel] ?? req.route.path`, by crafting a request path equal to `__proto__` or `constructor`, causing the spread `...(headers.path[path] ?? {})` to merge prototype properties into `mergedHeaders` and overwrite security-critical response headers like `Content-Security-Policy`?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user suppress security response headers (e.g., `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`) by influencing `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` to contain a conflicting key, since the spread on line 37 `...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})` has the highest precedence and overwrites both `headers.default` and `headers.path[path]` values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user set an arbitrary `Content-Type` response header (e.g., `text/html`) by influencing `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` to include `content-type: text/html`, since line 37 spreads `responseHeadersLabel` with highest priority, and then line 50 checks `contentType === APPLICATION_JSON` after the headers are set, causing the raw `responseData` (potentially containing HTML) to be sent without JSON encoding, enabling XSS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a `TypeError` crash by sending a request that results in `res.locals.responseData` being set to `null` (not `undefined`), since the guard on line 28 only checks `=== undefined` and not `null`, causing line 42 `res.locals.responseData.links?.next` to throw a `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null` that bypasses normal error handling?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a response to be sent twice (double-send) by exploiting the fact that `next()` is called on line 60 after `res.send()` on line 51, potentially triggering a subsequent middleware that also calls `res.send()`, leading to `Cannot set headers after they are sent` errors that crash the Node.js process or corrupt responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause an unhandled promise rejection by triggering an exception after `res.send()` has already been called (e.g., in the logging block on lines 53-58), since `responseHandler` is `async` and any thrown error after the response is sent may not be properly caught by Express error handlers, leading to process instability?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user leak internal timing information via the `elapsed` value logged on line 57, where `elapsed = Date.now() - startTime`, by sending many requests and observing which ones appear in logs (when `code >= 400`), enabling timing side-channel attacks to infer backend processing complexity or database query patterns?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause `res.locals[responseBodyLabel]` to store a reference to the full `responseData` object in memory (line 50), and if a subsequent middleware reads `responseBodyLabel` from `res.locals`, could this expose sensitive data that was intended only for the current response to other request contexts in a shared-state scenario?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[requestPathLabel] ?? req.route.path` fallback on line 33 by crafting a request where `requestPathLabel` is set to a path that exists as a key in `headers.path` config (e.g., `/api/v1/accounts`), overriding the actual route's cache-control headers and receiving a more permissive caching policy than intended?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a `ReferenceError: logger is not defined` crash by sending a request that reaches line 57 before the global `logger` is initialized (race condition during startup), since `logger` is a global variable set in `config.js` and `responseHandler` references it without a null check, leading to a denial-of-service during the startup window?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause `JSONStringify` (which is `JSONBig.stringify`) to throw an exception or produce unexpected output by crafting a response payload containing circular references, since `JSONStringify` on line 50 is called on `responseData` without a try-catch, and an unhandled exception here would propagate as an unhandled async error?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a memory exhaustion denial-of-service by triggering responses with extremely large `responseData` objects, since `JSONStringify(responseData)` on line 50 serializes the entire object synchronously in the event loop, blocking all other requests and potentially consuming gigabytes of memory for a single response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user inject JavaScript into a `Link` header value by supplying a `links.next` URL containing `javascript:` scheme characters, since `linkNextHeaderValue` only wraps the value in angle brackets (`<${linksNext}>`) without URL scheme validation, potentially enabling header-based XSS in clients that parse and render Link headers?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the `mergedHeaders` object to contain attacker-controlled values by exploiting the three-way spread merge `{...headers.default, ...(headers.path[path] ?? {}), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}` on lines 34-38, where the last spread always wins, allowing any downstream code that sets `responseHeadersLabel` based on user input to override security headers?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause `res.status(code)` on line 43 to set an unexpected HTTP status code by influencing `res.locals.statusCode` (line 41) through a route handler that reflects user-supplied values without validation, enabling the attacker to force a `200 OK` response for an error condition and confuse monitoring systems?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the `Link` header to be set with a value that bypasses browser CORS or CSP policies by injecting a `links.next` value pointing to an attacker-controlled domain, since `linkNextHeaderValue` performs no origin validation before constructing `<${linksNext}>; rel=\

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `Math.random() <= sampleRate` condition on line 56 to infer the server's `sampleRate` configuration value by sending many non-error requests and statistically analyzing which ones appear in logs (via timing or error correlation), leaking internal configuration details?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a `TypeError` by sending a request where `req.route` is `undefined` (e.g., for unmatched routes before the `responseData` check), since line 33 accesses `req.route.path` without a null check on `req.route`, and if `responseData` is somehow set but `req.route` is null, this would throw before the response is sent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: '] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user cause log entries to contain sensitive data by crafting a `req.originalUrl` that includes authentication tokens or PII in query parameters, since line 57 logs the full `req.originalUrl` without redacting sensitive query parameters, leading to credential exposure in log files?
